judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 answered better: it caught both the cross-tenant leak and the nightly_export breaking-change regression, while Report 1 missed the export caller defect entirely.

report_1.evidence: `search_appointments(session, tenant_id, q, status)` receives `tenant_id` as an argument but never uses it... There is no `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` clause anywhere. Every other read path in this codebase... goes through `build_appointment_query`, whose entire purpose... is to filter `Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`.

report_2.evidence: `tenant_id` is passed into `search_appointments(session, tenant_id, q, status)` but is **never referenced in the SQL** — there is no `tenant_id = :tenant_id` predicate anywhere... any authenticated caller from any tenant hitting `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=...` gets back matching appointments... from **every tenant in the database**

The PR changes `build_appointment_query`'s signature from `(session, tenant_id)` to `(session, tenant_id, filters)`, with `filters` as a **required** positional argument (no default value)... `backend/app/exports.py` — the nightly per-tenant CSV export job — is **not touched by this PR** and still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id).all()` with only two arguments... raises `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`

report_1.evidence: In this specific case I verified it is **not currently exploitable**: `status` is typed as `AppointmentStatus` (`backend/app/schemas.py`), a `str, Enum` restricted to exactly `booked`, `cancelled`, `completed`... the `q` parameter (the actual free-text user input) is correctly bound via the `:q` placeholder

report_2.evidence: `status` is typed as `AppointmentStatus`, a closed `str, Enum` (`backend/app/schemas.py`) with only `booked`/`cancelled`/`completed` as members, and FastAPI validates/rejects any other value before the request reaches this function — so this specific interpolation is not currently exploitable for injection via the API. I list it only as a bad-practice/defense-in-depth nit, not as a second blocking vulnerability
